titleEn: It Profits Not in My Creed

verses.ajuz: ـسَ بِصَوْتِ البَشِيرِ فِي كُلِّ نَادِ
ـبَ فَأَيْنَ القُبُورُ مِنْ عَهْدِ عَادِ

verses.sadr: رُبَّ لَحْدٍ قَدْ صَارَ لَحْدًا مِرَارًا
سِرْ إِنِ اسْطَعْتَ فِي الهَوَاءِ رُوَيْدًا

translationEn: It profits not, in my creed and belief, the wailing of a weeper nor the singing of one who rejoices. The voice of the death-announcer, when measured against the voice of the bearer of glad tidings, sounds alike in every assembly. Did that dove weep or sing upon the swaying tip of its branch? O friend, these graves of ours fill the open land — so where are the graves from the age of 'Ad? Tread lightly, for I think the surface of the earth is nothing but these bodies. Many a grave has become a grave again and again, laughing at the crowding of opposites. And one buried upon the remains of another buried, through the long ages and eternities. Walk, if you can, gently through the air — not strutting upon the remains of God's servants.
context: The opening of al-Ma'arri's famous elegy known as "Tread Lightly" (khaffif al-wat'a), composed upon the death of a jurist. It became one of the most celebrated philosophical poems in Arabic literature. The line "tread lightly, for I think the surface of the earth is nothing but these bodies" is one of the most quoted verses in all of Arabic poetry, expressing al-Ma'arri's distinctive blend of pessimism and humanism.

verses.ajuz: ـجَبُ إِلَّا مِنْ رَاغِبٍ فِي ازْدِيَادِ

verses.sadr: إِنَّ حُزْنًا فِي سَاعَةِ المَوْتِ أَضْعَا

translationEn: All of life is toil — what wonder is there except at one who desires more of it? The sorrow at the hour of death is many times the joy at the hour of birth. People's day is their evening — so does the one whose dawn has come have any morning ahead? People were created for eternity, yet a nation has gone astray thinking they were made for perishing.
context: From al-Ma'arri's Luzumiyyat (self-imposed double rhyme constraints). This poem captures his characteristic philosophical pessimism about worldly life. Despite his dark view of earthly existence, the final verse reveals a belief in the afterlife, showing the complexity of his thought.

titleAr: وَلَمَّا رَأَيْتُ الجَهْلَ فِي النَّاسِ فَاشِيًا
titleEn: When I Saw Ignorance Spreading Among People

verses.sadr: وَلَمَّا رَأَيْتُ الجَهْلَ فِي النَّاسِ فَاشِيًا
فَوَا عَجَبًا كَمْ يَدَّعِي الفَضْلَ نَاقِصٌ
verses.ajuz: وَوَا أَسَفًا كَمْ يُظْهِرُ النَّقْصَ فَاضِلُ

translationEn: When I saw ignorance spreading among people, I feigned ignorance until they thought I was ignorant. How wondrous that so many lacking claim virtue, and how lamentable that so many virtuous display their faults. It is as if the miser Madir accuses the generous Tayy of stinginess, and the stammerer Baqil faults the eloquent Quss for poor speech. O you who kindle a fire for one seeking its light — come to it, for the path lies straight before you.
context: A celebrated poem from al-Ma'arri's Luzumiyyat expressing his disillusionment with society. The references to Madir (proverbially stingy) and Baqil (proverbially inarticulate) versus Tayy (proverbially generous tribe) and Quss (proverbially eloquent) showcase his deep knowledge of Arabic literary culture.

titleEn: Let No Clouds Rain upon Me

verses.sadr: وَلَا تَحْسَبُوا قَوْلِي لَكُمْ شَتْمَ حَاقِدٍ

translationEn: Let no clouds rain upon me or upon my land — clouds that do not encompass all lands equally. When they ask about my doctrine, their question has a hidden motive — they seek through questioning to entrap. Though I be the last in time, I shall bring forth what the ancients could not achieve. Wake up, wake up, O you who have gone astray — your religions are but schemes of the ancients. Do not consider my words to you the abuse of one who bears a grudge, but I have offered counsel without harboring discord.
context: One of al-Ma'arri's most controversial poems from the Luzumiyyat, expressing his radical skepticism about organized religion. Al-Ma'arri, the blind philosopher-poet of Ma'arra, lived as a recluse and vegetarian. His skeptical views made him both celebrated and condemned across the centuries. He insisted his criticism came from sincere counsel, not hostility.

titleEn: This One Whom His Mother Taught

verses.sadr: هَذَا مَقَامُ مَنِ اسْتَوْحَشَ مِنْ أَرَبِ
verses.ajuz: الدُّنْيَا فَأَصْبَحَ لَا يَرْجُو وَلَا يَثِبُ
يَشْفِي السَّقِيمَ وَلَا يَشْفِي لَهُ سَقَمَا

translationEn: This is the station of one who grew wary of the world's desires, so he came to neither hope nor leap. My true friend is one who shares my sorrows and sheds his tears when my tears flow for my grief. It is madness — yet am I, in what is said of the sane, considered mad? Death showed me one single path, and I marveled at the multitude of paths. Youth, leisure, and wealth are a corruption for man — what a corruption! The physician of people is afflicted with their diseases; he cures the sick but cannot cure his own sickness.
context: A collection of al-Ma'arri's most penetrating aphoristic verses from both Saqt al-Zand and the Luzumiyyat. The verse about youth, leisure, and wealth being corrupting is widely quoted in Arabic moral literature. Al-Ma'arri's philosophical pessimism combined with sharp wit made him a unique voice in Arabic letters — often compared to European skeptics centuries before they appeared.
